topic: Petition of Brian Webb: Adjust jury fees to align with the minimum wage
description: Petition of Brian Webb: Adjust jury fees to align with the minimum wage Ministry of Justice Jemma McClymont, Policy Advisor, Courts System Policy Ben Sims, Manager, Courts System Policy Richard Williams, Regional Manager, Lower North Petition of Chained Dog Awareness NZ Trust: Mandate the Desexing of Dogs in NZ Except to Registered Breeders Petitioners Christine Breen & Sarah Shorter Ministry for Primary Industries Fiona Duncan, Director Regulatory Systems Policy Gavin Romayne, Manager Animal Welfare Policy SPCA Dr Alison Vaughan Dogs NZ Victoria Nelson, Chief Executive Owen Dance, Incoming President NZ Veterinary Association Becky Murphy, President Companion Animal Veterinarians Branch (on behalf of NZVA)

I'm Christine, and with me is Sarah and I'm, I'm kind of reading this out. Um, new Zealand's population of dogs is increasing and rescue groups are drowning and cannot keep up. Dogs can have two to three litters per year with five to 16 pups per litter.

With average litter sizes at nine to 10 pups. Dog numbers are growing at a faster rate than human numbers. In May, 2020, there were 851,000 dogs in New Zealand. Per the companion animals report, 70% of dogs were registered leaving 30% unregistered with the majority of these 250,000 plus dogs not being desexed.

Now in 2 0 2 5, although no official statistics, we've been told by animal control there would be one to 1.4 million dogs. Meaning 30% of these unregistered dogs equates to over 300,000 Essex dogs in New Zealand. Auckland Council advised yesterday they have a pilot program where they expect to desex 400 to 450 dogs across a few 10 or 10 South Auckland suburbs.

This is great, but it's just a drop in the ocean. At the heart of the problem is the failure to address the root cause. Desing. Without it uncontrolled dog populations lead to a devastating cycle. Dogs roaming, sometimes aggressive and attacking puppies, abandon rescues pounds.

And the SPCA overwhelmed, we've all seen the news stories and tragically too many dogs euthanized because there is no other option in practice. And that is Desex. Auckland Council, as an example, killed over 4,000 dogs in the last financial year.

That number is three and a half times higher than three years earlier. 35,000 complaints were taken by Auckland Council and the public made 80% more welfare complaints about abandoned and mistreated dogs. In the previous year, mana Cow count pound killed 173 dogs in one week.

Recently they killed dogs three times a week with other Auckland pounds killing once to twice a week. So a constant ongoing large scale euthanasia of healthy dogs due to the dog population being out of control, stopping unwanted litters before they start through mandatory sexing, we stop the cycle of unwanted puppies and thousands of dogs being killed.

This is backed by clear evidence and other countries are demonstrating the way forward. Some have attempted to blame the current COVID crisis or the current crisis on COVID, but this is not accurate. The overpopulation issue predates the pandemic and has been building for years.

What we are seeing now as a result of long term under investment in des sexing programs and a lack of enforceable re legislation. There's been significant demographic change in New Zealand over the last 25 years since chain dog awareness began. In many communities, there is strong resistance to des sexing male dogs, and the bulk of these families just love having puppies.

There are cultural and social beliefs that education alone won't shift. And third generational. Now, uh, we wanna acknowledge and thank the other submitters. Ministry for primary industries acknowledges the benefits des sexing can provide dogs, owners and communities. And mandatory des sexing of dogs would reduce the number of unwanted companion animals.

The SPCA supports mandatory des dissecting as an urgent and practical step to reduce the burden on themselves. Pounds and rescues. The unregulated and irresponsible breeding of dogs are significant welfare impacts contributing to overpopulation and community nuisance and danger Title regulation through breeder licensing and an updated code of welfare for dogs is needed.

Other countries such as Australia and the UK have implemented, implemented successful regulations. As such, the New Zealand Veterinary Association recognizes the importance of des sexing dogs not intended for breeding dogs. New Zealand agrees with the substance of our petition and that New Zealand has high levels of unwanted, dumped, abandoned, and abused dogs, and their lit of papas leading to animal welfare issues.

They identify the problem is irresponsible dog ownership and agree with implementing a breeder licensing program. Mandatory sexing reduces the huge number of dog attacks and makes our communities safer. As an example, between January and the 22nd of May this year, 2 0 2 5 Auckland Council received 6,167 reports of roaming dogs and impounded 2,460 dogs.

The number of dog crack attacks across Auckland continues to arise with 3000 attacks on people in animals reported in 2024 alone. The trauma to those attacked and the a CC costs continue to soar. For example, in 2021, the a CC costs for dog related injuries were 23.4 million.

In May, 2024, Auckland Council announced there were 27,000 dog related injuries reported every year in New Zealand with just under 3000 of those to the babies and children under 14 years of age. As an example, in Victoria and Australia, annual city council's free cat sterilization program led to an 82% reduction in euthanasia and a 66% decrease in empowerments Over eight years.

A public des sexing fund needs to be established with mobile des sexing units being set up in low socioeconomic suburbs when many have little or no access to their own transport. The majority of dogs in low socioeconomic environments live outside, not allowed inside, let alone in a vehicle if there is one to go to.

A vet mandatory de sexing helps councils better arrange their resources by reducing the exhausting cycle of impounds and euthanasias. Uh, it is better to desex dogs than to keep killing them, to reduce the numbers. Thank you. Alright, thank you very much.

So as you know, there are two mainly pieces of legislation involved with the management of dogs in New Zealand. So the Dog Control Act mostly delivered by local government and the Animal Welfare Act.

Um, under the Animal Welfare Act, there's the code of welfare for dogs that, um, that we have some responsibility for. So the purpose of the Dog Control Act is to provide control for got dogs, that's where all the registration occurs. And limit damage caused by dogs to either property, which includes animals or people rather than improving the welfare of the animals or the all population control.

So they don't have a population control purpose. And the purpose of the Animal Welfare Act is to ensure that the owners of animals and persons in charge of animals attend properly to the welfare of those animals and to ensure physical health and behavioral needs of an animal are met by the owner or in person in charge of that animal. Um, the code of welfare for dogs acknowledges the benefits of des sexing dogs and, um, the benefits that it provides for dogs, dog owners and the communities.

And it includes a recommended best practice that says dogs are not intended for breeding dogs, not intended for breeding, should be desexed before puberty. And we acknowledge this, do outweigh the negatives and include desexed animals live significantly longer than those that are not. The removal of hormonal influences can contribute to that can contribute to inter dog aggression and roaming as a benefit and the reduction or elimination of diseases, including infections in cancers of the reproductive system.

So Desex has all of those benefits, but it does not in and of itself constitute a welfare issue. So Des sexing doesn't constitute a welfare issue in and of itself under the current legislation, under the current legislation. And nor is it considered necessary to meet physical health and behavioral needs of an animal.

So it doesn't, as it's currently drafted, uh, it doesn't enable NPI to consider population control to improve welfare outcomes for animals. So it's about, it's very much about the needs of an individual animal rather than the impact of population control on wider welfare benefits.

We appreciate the concerns raised by chain dogs awareness to New Zealand trust and commend longstanding commitment to animal welfare. We agree that dog of population remains a significant concern.

Rescue organizations and shelters across the country are under immense pressure with some having to make the decision to euthanize healthy dogs because they run out of space and resources. It's not acceptable, and we do believe more needs to be done. However, uh, the NZVA do not support the proposal to mandate des sexing of all privately owned dogs.

Uh, I'll explain why. Um, but firstly, I need to explain a little bit of the terms that I will be using, um, because some of it's sort of new even to our vet world. So sterilization means that the dog is unable to reproduce. Des sexing means that the animal has been sterilized, so it can't reproduce by the surgical removal of its gonads, so its ovaries or its testes in a male.

When we remove gonads, we also remove a source of hormones, and so the dog goes into an abnormal state of hormone balance. There is growing evidence worldwide to suggest that this all, that it is this hormone imbalance that contributes to some specific health problems in dogs which have been desexed. A dog can also be sterilized, so not able to reproduce, but still have their ovaries and testes and their hormones produced by them, by removing the uterus for the female, which is a hysterectomy or tying off the RIN in the male, which is a vasectomy.

Traditionally, dogs have been sterilized by des sexing worldwide, but particularly in New Zealand. But there is a growing movement toward hormone preserving sterilization so that the animal cannot reproduce, but still has the beneficial effects of normal hormone levels. The petition suggests that New Zealand dog population is soaring and approaching 1 million, but this isn't supported by the best available data companion animals.

New Zealand estimated their dog population around 850,000 in 2020. Their latest estimate from 2024 is actually slightly lower at 830,000. The National Dog database tracking all registered dogs shows an increase of only 2,900 dogs between 2021 and 2023.

That's just a rise of 0.5% in over two years. We acknowledge that shelters are overwhelmed, but it's important to base policy on sound data and the data does not support the claim that we are seeing explosive growth in the overall dog population. The NZVA supports the principle that dogs not intended for breeding should be sterilized at some point.

Sterilization remains one of the most effective ways to prevent unwanted litters and contributes to population control. It can also provide certain health benefits and behavioral benefits, such as reducing risk of memory, cancer and females it performed before their second season. However, we strongly believe that desex decisions must be tailored to the individual animal blanket mandates are too blunt an instrument for what is in practice a very complex decision.

For example, en large and giant breed dogs, they're more prone to orthopedic issues and early sterilization with removal of sex hormones can increase those risks. In many cases, delaying des sexing may be in the dog's best interest from a health perspective. There is growing evidence to support this. And indeed, the world's small animal veterinary association reproduction control guidelines, which were just published in 2023, support this strongly.

The veterinary profession is guided by evidence-based medicine, and the literature is clear that one size fits all approach to des sexing can have serious unintended consequences to the individual dogs. We believe des Sexing should be done at the right time for the right reasons, and that requires a conversation between the owner and the veterinarian.

So what do we suggest? We believe that the focus should be on targeted, effective measures, addressing the root cause of dog overpopulation without infringing on responsible ownership and individual dog health. Specifically, NZVA supports mandatory sterilization of unowned dogs and puppies being re-homed by sheltered shelters, rescue groups and councils.

These organizations are doing vital work under pressure and should be supported with clear policy preventing further reproduction of dogs or ready, contributing to the population burden. Uh, two, improve regulation of dog breeding. We recommend that all breeders be required to obtain, to obtain a permit exemption should only be granted to breeders who are registered with an organization that enforces strict ethical breeding standards, including limits on litter numbers, health screening, and lifetime rehoming support.

Unregulated backyard breeding, which often contributes to the most shelter overflows must be brought under control. Three community level desex initiatives, particularly in high need or low income areas to support responsible peer ownership. And four, investment in education, helping New Zealanders to understand what responsible dog ownership looks like, why sterilization of dogs matters, and how to prevent accidental litters.

We would also point to the committee, the committee to successful International examples. Countries like Sweden and Switzerland are often cited for the excellent companion animal welfare outcomes. Interestingly, these countries do not actually even allow routine des sexing. It's actually banned in many cases due to those countries, deeming it as an unnecessary surgical procedure.

Instead, they've succeeded through robust breeding regulation, high expectations of owner responsibility and strong public education and access to veterinary care. That faucet. A culture where dogs are treated as family abandonment is rare and population control is achieved through better systems, not through surgery. So in closing, uh, we support the intent behind this petition.

We all want fewer unwanted dogs and better welfare outcomes, but mandatory des dissecting of all owned dogs is not the right tool for the job. Instead, we ask the committee to consider a nuanced veterinary lead approach to sterilization on an individual animal basis, but targeted mandatory sterilization for unowned dogs, tighter regulation of breeding practices, investment in education and access to veterinary services.
